event: Early Archaic (pre-ceramic) peoples reached the Caribbean islands, with evidence suggesting human presence in the Lesser Antilles region.
Arawak-speaking Saladoid peoples migrated northward from South America and settled the island they called Hairouna or Youloumain.
Kalinago (Island Carib) peoples, migrating from the South American mainland, displaced or absorbed the earlier Arawak inhabitants of the island.
Christopher Columbus is traditionally said to have sighted the island, reputedly on Saint Vincent's feast day, lending it the name Saint Vincent.
African survivors of shipwrecked or captured slave ships intermarried with the Kalinago, giving rise to the Garifuna or 'Black Caribs'.
England and France signed a treaty declaring Saint Vincent a neutral island left to the Caribs, reflecting fierce indigenous resistance to colonization.
French settlers from Martinique began establishing plantations on Saint Vincent, cultivating coffee, tobacco, indigo, cotton and sugar with enslaved African labor.
France ceded Saint Vincent to Britain under the Treaty of Paris ending the Seven Years' War.
British attempts to survey and seize Carib lands sparked open conflict with the Garifuna led by Chief Joseph Chatoyer.
The First Carib War ended with a treaty between Britain and the Garifuna establishing a boundary between their territories.
France recaptured Saint Vincent during the American War of Independence with Carib support.
The Treaty of Versailles returned Saint Vincent to British control.
The Second Carib War erupted as the Garifuna, aided by the French, rose against British rule.
Garifuna paramount chief Joseph Chatoyer, a national hero of Saint Vincent, was killed during the Second Carib War.
Britain defeated the Garifuna and deported some 5,000 'Black Caribs' to the island of Roatan off Honduras, founding the Central American Garifuna diaspora.
The La Soufriere volcano erupted violently, devastating plantations and causing widespread destruction across the island.
Slavery was abolished throughout the British Empire, freeing the enslaved population of Saint Vincent under an apprenticeship system.
Full emancipation took effect, ending the apprenticeship period for formerly enslaved Vincentians.
Portuguese laborers from Madeira began arriving as indentured workers to address labor shortages on the sugar estates.
Indentured laborers from India began arriving to work on Saint Vincent's plantations following the decline of the sugar economy.
Saint Vincent became part of the federated British Windward Islands colony.
La Soufriere erupted catastrophically, killing around 1,600 people, just hours before the deadlier Mont Pelee eruption on nearby Martinique.
A new constitution established a partly elected Legislative Council, giving Vincentians limited representation.
Riots over economic hardship and falling wages erupted in Kingstown, part of a wave of labor unrest across the British West Indies.
Universal adult suffrage was introduced, granting all adult Vincentians the right to vote.
Saint Vincent joined the short-lived West Indies Federation.
The West Indies Federation collapsed, ending hopes of a unified independent British Caribbean state.
Saint Vincent attained associated statehood with Britain, gaining full internal self-government while Britain retained control of defense and foreign affairs.
La Soufriere erupted in April, forcing the evacuation of thousands shortly before the country's independence.
Saint Vincent and the Grenadines gained full independence from the United Kingdom on 27 October, with Milton Cato as first Prime Minister.
Saint Vincent and the Grenadines was admitted as a member state of the United Nations and the Commonwealth of Nations.
Hurricane Allen devastated the banana crop, the country's economic mainstay, causing severe hardship.
James Mitchell of the New Democratic Party became Prime Minister, beginning a long period of NDP governance.
Saint Vincent and the Grenadines expanded its offshore financial services sector as it sought to diversify beyond agriculture.
Ralph Gonsalves of the Unity Labour Party became Prime Minister, beginning a long tenure in office.
Voters rejected a referendum to replace the monarchy with a republic and adopt a new constitution.
Devastating Christmas Eve floods caused deadly landslides and widespread damage across the islands.
Argyle International Airport opened, providing the country its first airport capable of handling large jets and direct international flights.
Saint Vincent and the Grenadines was elected to a two-year term on the United Nations Security Council, becoming the smallest nation ever to serve on it.
La Soufriere erupted explosively in April, forcing the evacuation of around 20,000 people and burying farmland in ash.
Hurricane Beryl, an early and exceptionally powerful storm, struck the Grenadines, causing catastrophic damage on Union, Canouan and Mayreau islands.
